what-is-toccata.title: What is Toccata?
what-is-toccata.description: Kaspa transitions from a high-speed monetary network into a programmable computational system.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.reality_shift: Toccata is not simply a feature upgrade. It is the moment Kaspa transitions from a high-speed monetary network into a programmable computational system. Before Toccata, Kaspa could transfer value extremely efficiently, but coins themselves remained passive. They could move between addresses, but they could not enforce behavior, verify external computation, or participate in complex applications. Toccata changes this.
sections.mental_model: Before Toccata, Kaspa was like a perfectly optimized railway system capable of moving value extremely quickly, but incapable of running applications. Toccata adds programmable infrastructure directly into the tracks themselves. Now value movement can carry logic, conditions, proofs, and application state.
sections.mechanism: Toccata is a consensus-level hard fork introducing SilverScript covenant programming, KIP-10 introspection opcodes, KIP-17 byte primitives, native Groth16 verification, native RISC Zero STARK verification, and KIP-21 partitioned sequencing. These additions enable programmable UTXOs, based ZK applications, and trustless off-chain computation verification. Unlike traditional account-based virtual machines, Toccata preserves Kaspa's UTXO architecture while extending it with covenant logic and proof-based execution.
sections.implications: Toccata enables decentralized exchanges, programmable vaults, escrow systems, based rollups, verifiable off-chain computation, privacy-preserving applications, and atomic multi-contract execution. Applications become possible directly on Kaspa's base layer.
sections.constraints: Toccata does not turn Kaspa into a general-purpose shared-state virtual machine. The system remains intentionally constrained: state evolves through UTXO transitions, execution is bounded by script limits, complex computation happens off-chain, and verification remains deterministic and compact. These constraints are deliberate. They preserve scalability and security.
sections.system_position: Toccata is the foundation layer that all later application infrastructure depends on. Without covenant introspection and proof verification, based execution systems cannot exist.
sections.insight: Toccata expands what computation can do on Kaspa without requiring Kaspa itself to execute all computation.

sections.title: The Architecture of Toccata
sections.reality_shift: Toccata can be understood as a layered computational system where each layer performs a specific role. SilverScript defines programmable rules. Covenants enforce state transitions. ZK applications execute heavy computation off-chain. Proof systems verify correctness. The canonical bridge connects off-chain execution to L1 settlement. Kaspa consensus orders and finalizes everything atomically.
sections.mental_model: Think of Toccata as a computer where the heavy lifting happens in specialized external processors, but the motherboard verifies every result before accepting it. The Kaspa base layer is the motherboard — it doesn't run the programs itself, but it cryptographically confirms they ran correctly.
sections.mechanism: Computation expands outward while verification remains compact and deterministic. Kaspa nodes do not execute arbitrary application logic globally. Instead, they verify cryptographic guarantees that the logic was executed correctly. This preserves scalability while enabling complex applications.
sections.insight: Computation expands outward while verification remains compact and deterministic. Kaspa nodes do not execute arbitrary application logic globally. Instead, they verify cryptographic guarantees that the logic was executed correctly.

silverscript.title: SilverScript
silverscript.description: The covenant language that turns passive UTXOs into programmable state machines.

sections.title: What is SilverScript?
sections.reality_shift: Kaspa coins can now enforce behavior instead of simply storing value. A coin can define conditions for how it may be spent, when it may move, where it may go, and what transaction structure must exist around it.
sections.mental_model: A normal cryptocurrency coin behaves like digital cash. Once someone controls it, they can spend it freely. A SilverScript covenant behaves more like a locked container with embedded rules. Even the owner must obey the conditions encoded into the coin.
sections.mechanism: SilverScript is a high-level covenant language compiling to native Kaspa Script. It uses KIP-10 introspection opcodes, KIP-17 byte primitives, and covenant state transitions through UTXO chains. The resulting bytecode lives directly inside the ScriptPubKey of covenant UTXOs. When a spend attempt occurs, the covenant inspects the transaction and validates whether the transition is allowed. If validation fails, the transaction is rejected.
sections.implications: SilverScript enables timelocked vaults, multisig coordination, escrow systems, programmable custody, state machines, and application-specific transaction rules. Every programmable system in Toccata begins with covenant-controlled UTXOs.
sections.constraints: SilverScript intentionally avoids unrestricted shared mutable global state. This creates important limitations: contracts evolve through transaction chains, state synchronization is explicit, and some patterns common in EVM systems behave differently. However, this isolation dramatically reduces entire categories of exploits. A failure inside one covenant cannot corrupt unrelated application state.
sections.system_position: SilverScript is the behavioral layer of Toccata. It defines how programmable state transitions occur. ZK applications, state UTXOs, and based execution all depend on covenant infrastructure.
sections.insight: SilverScript turns UTXOs from passive outputs into self-enforcing state machines.

covenants.title: Covenants
covenants.description: Self-enforcing spending rules that turn coins into programmable state objects.

sections.title: What Are Covenants?
sections.reality_shift: A coin can now restrict not only who may spend it, but how it may be spent. This changes coins from static balances into programmable state objects.
sections.mental_model: A covenant is like a contract physically attached to a coin. The rules travel with the value itself. Every future transaction must satisfy those rules or the network rejects the spend.
sections.mechanism: Using KIP-10 introspection opcodes, covenant scripts can inspect outputs, amounts, fees, block height, transaction structure, and other contextual data. This enables covenant-controlled state transitions where each UTXO represents a stage in a protocol. Each valid spend creates the next valid state.
sections.implications: Covenants enable programmable vaults, enforced delays, stateful applications, decentralized coordination systems, and trust-minimized financial logic. They provide the foundation for higher-level execution systems.
sections.constraints: Covenants do not create unrestricted global memory. State propagation must occur through transaction flows. This introduces architectural constraints but preserves strong isolation guarantees.
sections.system_position: Covenants are the state transition engine of Toccata. They define how application state evolves securely across time.
sections.insight: A covenant is not just a spending rule — it is a programmable state transition boundary.

zk-applications.title: ZK Applications
zk-applications.description: Off-chain computation with on-chain verification through zero-knowledge proofs.

sections.title: What Are ZK Applications?
sections.reality_shift: Complex computation can happen outside the blockchain while Kaspa still verifies that the result is correct. This allows applications to scale without forcing every node to execute every computation.
sections.mental_model: Instead of redoing the work, the network verifies a cryptographic receipt proving the work was done correctly. Kaspa validates correctness without replaying the entire computation.
sections.mechanism: Applications execute computation off-chain. They then generate Groth16 proofs or RISC Zero STARK proofs. These proofs are submitted to Kaspa where native verifier opcodes validate them deterministically. The canonical bridge then allows verified proofs to authorize state transitions on-chain.
sections.implications: ZK applications enable scalable execution, privacy-preserving systems, complex computation, trustless application settlement, based rollup infrastructure, and efficient state verification. Heavy execution moves outward while consensus verification remains lightweight.
sections.constraints: Proof generation itself can still be computationally expensive. Different proving systems involve tradeoffs: Groth16 provides compact proofs but requires trusted setup; STARKs avoid trusted setup but produce larger proofs. Applications must balance flexibility, proof size, latency, and proving cost.
sections.system_position: ZK applications are the computational expansion layer of Toccata. They extend execution capacity without burdening consensus with full computation replay.
sections.insight: Computation becomes effectively unbounded while verification remains compact.

zk-opcodes.title: ZK Opcodes
zk-opcodes.description: Native proof verifiers built directly into Kaspa Script.

sections.title: Groth16 Verifier
sections.reality_shift: Kaspa can now verify succinct zero-knowledge proofs directly inside transactions.
sections.mental_model: A Groth16 proof is like an extremely compressed mathematical certificate. The network can verify correctness in milliseconds without re-executing the original computation.
sections.mechanism: Toccata introduces a native Groth16 verification opcode. The opcode performs elliptic curve pairing checks directly at the script level. This eliminates the need for large external verifier contracts.
sections.implications: Groth16 enables efficient proof verification, private state transitions, compact trustless computation, and low-cost validity systems.
sections.constraints: Groth16 requires trusted setup ceremonies for each proving circuit. Its flexibility is lower than general-purpose proving systems.
sections.system_position: Groth16 provides high-efficiency proof verification for specialized applications.
sections.insight: Groth16 minimizes verification cost by compressing complex computation into tiny proofs.

sections.title: RISC Zero STARK Verifier
sections.reality_shift: Kaspa gains the ability to verify arbitrary program execution cryptographically.
sections.mental_model: Instead of proving a single predefined circuit, STARK systems can prove that an entire program executed correctly.
sections.mechanism: RISC Zero compiles programs into the RISC‑V instruction set. Execution generates a STARK proof. Kaspa's native verifier opcode validates the proof directly on-chain.
sections.implications: This enables general-purpose verifiable computation, arbitrary off-chain execution, flexible application architecture, and trustless execution environments.
sections.constraints: STARK proofs are typically larger than Groth16 proofs. Proof generation can also require significant computational resources.
sections.system_position: RISC Zero verification expands Toccata from specialized proof systems into general-purpose verifiable computation.
sections.insight: STARK verification allows Kaspa to verify programs without directly executing them.

kip-specifications.title: KIP Specifications
kip-specifications.description: The formal Kaspa Improvement Proposals that define Toccata's technical standards.

sections.title: KIP-10: Introspection Opcodes
sections.reality_shift: UTXOs can now inspect the transaction attempting to spend them.
sections.mental_model: Before KIP-10, a coin was effectively blind. It could only check signatures. After KIP-10, the coin can inspect its surrounding transaction environment before approving a spend.
sections.mechanism: KIP-10 introduces opcodes enabling scripts to inspect inputs, outputs, fees, amounts, transaction structure, and contextual execution data. This enables programmable state transition logic.
sections.implications: KIP-10 enables covenants, canonical bridges, programmable spending rules, and application state transitions.
sections.constraints: Introspection remains intentionally bounded. The goal is programmable validation without introducing unrestricted execution complexity.
sections.system_position: KIP-10 acts as the sensory system of Toccata's covenant architecture.
sections.insight: KIP-10 gives UTXOs awareness of the transaction attempting to consume them.

sections.title: KIP-17: Byte Primitives
sections.reality_shift: Kaspa scripts can now manipulate structured data rather than only simple hashes and arithmetic.
sections.mental_model: KIP-17 gives covenant scripts the ability to handle raw information as composable building blocks.
sections.mechanism: KIP-17 introduces byte-level operations including concatenation, slicing, parsing, and serialization primitives. These operations allow scripts to process complex structured data.
sections.implications: KIP-17 enables structured covenant state, arrays and complex data layouts, richer application logic, and advanced state machine design.
sections.constraints: Script environments remain resource-bounded. KIP-17 expands expressiveness without turning Kaspa Script into a fully unrestricted execution engine.
sections.system_position: KIP-17 is the data-handling layer that allows higher-level covenant logic to exist.
sections.insight: KIP-17 allows covenants to reason about structured information instead of only static values.

sections.title: KIP-21: Partitioned Sequencing
sections.reality_shift: One application's activity no longer forces all other applications to share the same congestion curve.
sections.mental_model: Instead of one global traffic jam, applications operate in separated throughput lanes.
sections.mechanism: KIP-21 partitions sequencing commitments so proof verification costs scale relative to individual application activity. This isolates fee dynamics between applications.
sections.implications: KIP-21 enables scalable multi-application ecosystems, predictable proof verification costs, reduced fee spillover, and sustainable based execution architecture.
sections.constraints: Partitioning reduces shared congestion effects but does not eliminate physical throughput limits entirely. Applications still inherit base-layer ordering constraints.
sections.system_position: KIP-21 is the scalability isolation layer of Toccata's ZK architecture.
sections.insight: KIP-21 prevents local application demand from becoming global network congestion.

atomic-composability.title: Atomic Composability
atomic-composability.description: Multiple applications coordinating within a single transaction that fully succeeds or fully fails.

sections.title: What Is Atomic Composability?
sections.reality_shift: Multiple applications can coordinate within one transaction that either fully succeeds or fully fails.
sections.mental_model: Complex financial interactions behave like a single synchronized machine rather than fragmented asynchronous systems.
sections.mechanism: Because based applications inherit L1 ordering directly from Kaspa consensus, all participants observe the same canonical transaction sequence. This enables synchronous cross-application execution.
sections.implications: Atomic composability enables flash loans, synchronized DeFi interactions, multi-stage financial operations, and trust-minimized cross-application execution.
sections.constraints: Applications remain bounded by shared settlement ordering and execution constraints. Atomic guarantees require deterministic settlement conditions.
sections.system_position: Atomic composability is the coordination layer that allows independent applications to behave like a unified execution environment.
sections.insight: Toccata allows multiple systems to settle as one indivisible state transition.

canonical-bridge.title: The Canonical Bridge
canonical-bridge.description: How off-chain computation authorizes on-chain state transitions without trusted intermediaries.

sections.title: What Is the Canonical Bridge?
sections.reality_shift: Off-chain computation can directly authorize on-chain state transitions without trusted intermediaries.
sections.mental_model: The canonical bridge acts like a cryptographic checkpoint between computation and settlement. It does not trust the computation. It verifies proof that the computation followed the rules.
sections.mechanism: A State UTXO contains covenant logic validating ZK proofs. When off-chain execution completes: (1) a proof is generated, (2) the proof is submitted on-chain, (3) native verifier opcodes validate correctness, (4) the State UTXO transitions to a new state. The bridge therefore links external computation to L1 settlement cryptographically.
sections.implications: The canonical bridge enables trustless rollups, proof-authorized state transitions, based execution systems, and scalable application settlement.
sections.constraints: The bridge verifies correctness, not application quality. Security still depends on correct circuit design, proof generation, and covenant implementation.
sections.system_position: The canonical bridge is the settlement gateway between off-chain execution and Kaspa consensus.
sections.insight: The canonical bridge allows Kaspa to trust proofs instead of re-executing computation.

sections.title: General Questions
bullets: Toccata changes execution capabilities, not monetary policy. The 28.7B KAS supply cap remains unchanged.
Regular transfers remain lightweight. Programmable execution and ZK verification are isolated through bounded execution rules and partitioned sequencing.
Most normal users holding KAS in wallets do not need to take action. However, miners, node operators, and infrastructure providers must upgrade to Toccata-compatible software before activation.

Toccata introduces programmability, but through a fundamentally different architecture. Kaspa preserves UTXO-based state, deterministic verification, bounded execution, and proof-based scalability rather than adopting unrestricted shared-state execution.
sections.insight: Toccata is not an attempt to replicate existing smart contract systems. It is an attempt to build programmable computation around Kaspa's original scalability philosophy rather than against it.
